title: Water & Land Are Systems
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

competence_markers.description: The child can describe where water goes when it rains on their property, based on direct observation
The child understands that covered soil absorbs water and bare soil sheds water
The child can identify erosion and explain what causes it
The child has participated in rain capture (rain barrel or similar)
The child can describe at least one connection between water, soil, plants, and animals on the land

content.why_this_matters:
When it rains, where does the water go? This is one of the most important questions a person can ask about any piece of land, and you are going to learn to answer it.

Water is the most important thing on a farm, a garden, or any piece of land. More important than soil, more important than seeds, more important than anything else — because without water, nothing grows. Nothing. Every plant, every animal, every living thing in the soil needs water to survive.

The amazing thing is that water falls from the sky for free. Rain is the most valuable resource any piece of land receives, and it comes without anyone paying for it or ordering it. But what happens after it hits the ground makes all the difference. On well-managed land, water soaks into the soil, feeding plant roots and filling underground reserves. On poorly managed land, water runs off the surface, carrying topsoil with it and leaving the land drier than before it rained.

You are also going to learn to see the land as a system — a connected whole where everything affects everything else. Where the water goes affects what grows. What grows affects the soil. The soil affects how much water it can hold. The animals that graze the land affect the plants, which affect the soil, which affects the water. It is all connected, and learning to see those connections is one of the most valuable skills you will ever develop.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Water observation** — watching where water goes when it rains and understanding why it goes there
2. **Simple water capture** — the concept that rainwater can be collected and used
3. **Erosion awareness** — recognizing where soil is being washed away and understanding why
4. **Fence awareness** — understanding that fences manage where animals go, and where animals go shapes the land
5. **Systems thinking seeds** — the beginning of seeing land as a connected system, not a collection of separate parts
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Where Does the Water Go?

Next time it rains, go outside (with rain gear) and watch. This is your most important assignment in this packet.

Watch where the water goes. Does it soak into the ground? Does it puddle? Does it run across the surface? Where does the running water end up — does it flow into a ditch, a stream, a storm drain, or just off the edge of the property?

Now look at the differences:
- **Where the ground is covered with plants, mulch, or leaf litter**, water soaks in. The plants and ground cover slow the water down, giving it time to soak into the soil. The root channels and worm tunnels in the soil create paths for water to follow down into the earth.
- **Where the ground is bare — no plants, no cover**, water runs across the surface. It picks up speed on slopes. As it runs, it carries tiny particles of soil with it. This is erosion — the loss of topsoil to running water.
- **Where the ground is compacted — packed hard by feet, vehicles, or machinery**, water does not soak in at all. It sheets across the surface as if the ground were pavement.

**Key understanding:** Healthy, covered, uncompacted soil absorbs water. Bare, compacted soil sheds water. Every management decision that affects soil cover and compaction affects water management.

### Part 2: Catching Rain

Rain falls on the roof of every building. Most of the time, that water runs off the roof, through the gutters, and onto the ground — where it either soaks in or runs away. But it does not have to be lost.

**Rain barrels:** A barrel placed under a downspout catches rainwater from the roof. A single rain event on a small roof can fill a 55-gallon barrel. That water can be used for watering the garden — clean, free, and stored where you need it.

How much water falls on your roof when it rains one inch? Multiply the square footage of your roof by 0.623 to get the number of gallons. A 1,000 square-foot roof catches 623 gallons in one inch of rain. That is a lot of water — and in most places, it all runs away unused.

**Your activity:** Help set up a rain barrel under a downspout. Observe how quickly it fills during a rainstorm. Use the water to irrigate a garden bed. Understand that this is the simplest form of water capture — and that the same principle works at every scale, from a barrel to a pond.

### Part 3: Erosion — When the Land Washes Away

Walk around your property or a nearby piece of land after a rain. Look for places where water has carved small channels in the soil, where the ground has washed out along a path or road, or where muddy water is flowing off bare ground. These are signs of erosion.

Erosion is soil leaving. Every time topsoil washes away, the land loses its most fertile layer — the layer where organic matter, nutrients, and soil organisms are concentrated. It takes nature hundreds of years to build an inch of topsoil. A single heavy rainstorm on bare ground can carry inches of topsoil away in hours.

**What prevents erosion:**
- **Plant cover** — roots hold soil in place, and leaves and stems slow water down
- **Mulch** — organic material on the surface protects soil from raindrop impact (which dislodges particles) and slows runoff
- **Ground cover** — low-growing plants spread across the surface, protecting every inch of soil

**What causes erosion:**
- **Bare soil** — unprotected from rain and wind
- **Compaction** — hard surfaces that prevent infiltration
- **Slopes without cover** — gravity and water combine to carry soil downhill
- **Overgrazing** — when animals eat plants down to bare ground, the soil has no protection

### Part 4: Fences and Animals Shape the Land

You learned in 2.5.5-A that animals change the land they graze. Fences are the tools that control where animals go — and where animals go determines how the land is managed.

A pasture with no fences — where animals go wherever they want — will be grazed unevenly. Animals return to their favorite spots and eat those plants down to nothing while other areas grow tall and coarse. The overgrazed spots become bare, compacted, and eroded. The undergrazed spots waste their potential.

A pasture with fences that divide it into sections can be managed differently. Animals graze one section, then move to the next, and the first section rests and recovers. This is the basic idea of rotational grazing — which you will learn in detail at Weathering Storms. For now, understand: fences are not just for keeping animals in. Fences are land management tools.

### Part 5: Everything Is Connected

Look at your property — or any piece of land — and notice how everything connects:
- Rain falls on the land → water soaks in or runs off
- Where water soaks in → plants grow well, soil stays moist
- Where plants grow well → soil is covered and protected from erosion
- Where soil is protected → more water soaks in next time it rains
- This is a positive cycle — each good outcome strengthens the next

Now look at the negative cycle:
- Bare soil → water runs off → erosion carries topsoil away → less organic matter → soil holds less water → plants struggle → more bare soil
- This is a degradation cycle — each bad outcome makes the next one worse

Your job, as someone learning to manage land, is to create positive cycles and break negative ones. Everything you do — covering bare soil, building compost, managing where animals graze, catching rainwater — is either building the land up or letting it run down. There is no neutral.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Water Observation
**Developing well:** The child can describe where water goes on their property during rain. They can point out areas where water soaks in and areas where it runs off, and can suggest why the difference exists.

**Developing slowly:** The child has not observed water behavior during rain, or observed once but cannot describe patterns.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the rain observation walk happened? If not, schedule it for the next rain event. If it happened once, do it again — patterns become clearer with repetition.

### 2. Erosion Recognition
**Developing well:** The child can identify erosion evidence (channels, bare spots, exposed roots, deposited soil) and explain that bare soil washes away while covered soil does not.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot distinguish erosion from other landscape features.

**Diagnostic questions:** Walk the property after rain and point out specific erosion evidence. Ask: "What happened here? Why do you think the soil washed away here but not over there?" The guided observation builds the pattern.

### 3. Systems Thinking Seeds
**Developing well:** The child spontaneously connects land elements: "The chickens make the grass short, so the rain washes the soil" or "The mulch keeps the water in so the plants grow better." These connections may be simplified, but they show systems awareness.

**Developing slowly:** The child sees each element (water, soil, plants, animals) separately without connecting them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Draw a simple map together showing water flow, animal areas, garden, and compost. Then ask: "What happens to the compost when it rains? Where does the water from the chicken run go? What happens to the garden when we do not water?" The guided mapping builds connections.
